content.assessment: This structural split directly engages transport and capacity criteria by matching commute burden to age-appropriate travel capacity, but its geographic feasibility rests on whether village populations retain sufficient cohort size for even grades 1-4, given the ongoing school-age cohort shrinkage nationwide.
opportunities: Multi-village route-based transport logistics models (as used for minority-language school buses across 13 counties) offer a transferable operational template for the upper-grade consolidation leg, though direct applicability to domestic consolidation is unconfirmed
strengths: Explicitly targets the transport criterion by aligning younger children's proximity needs with documented harms of long rural commutes, while shifting only older students to consolidated sites
Provides a network resilience mechanism (formally preserved buildings) distinct from simple closure, potentially reducing outright network attrition
threats: Reversal risk analogous to Poland's 2017 rollback would be network-disruptive, particularly since Hungary's rural transport infrastructure gap makes any structural reorganization more fragile to reversal shocks than in better-connected systems
Continuing population decline could erode grade 1-4 cohorts below viability thresholds regardless of structural preservation, especially in villages already losing migration-driven population gains
weaknesses: Older students' commute burden is redistributed rather than eliminated, and rural public transport in North-Eastern, South-Western, and Western regions is documented as impractical for daily commuting
Retained lower-grade village schools remain exposed to national enrollment/class-size thresholds, so the geographic preservation may not be capacity-durable amid continuing population decline
